results.possibleIdentity: Walter Wright is a pioneer video artist, animator, and software engineer who has been active since the late 1960s. He is recognized as one of the first video animators, particularly for his work with the Scanimate analog computer system. Wright studied architecture at the University of Manitoba (graduating in 1965) and systems design at the University of Waterloo (graduating in 1967), where he began experimenting with computer-aided design and electronic music. In the early 1970s, he worked in New York City at Computer Image Corp and Dolphin Productions. During this period, he collaborated with Ed Emshwiller on influential early video works, including "Thermogenesis" and "Scapemates" (1971), the latter of which was the first computer graphics video nominated for an Emmy Award. Wright was an associate director at The Kitchen in New York and succeeded Nam June Paik as the artist-in-residence at the Experimental Television Center (ETC) from 1973 to 1976. At ETC, he pioneered video performance by touring with the Paik/Abe Video Synthesizer. His work was heavily inspired by the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) group and the research conducted at Bell Laboratories. Throughout his career, he has developed his own hardware and software for artistic use, such as the "Video Shredder," and has remained active in the performance of improvised video and sound.
